name: Dr. Cassandra Patel
key_publications: 'Ethical Frameworks for AI-Enabled Biological Research' (Nature, 2025); 'The Governance Gap in Synthetic Biology' (Science Policy, 2024)
interview_notes: Cassandra has been thinking about the intersection of AI and bio governance for 5+ years, before it was trendy. Her work on ethical frameworks is deeply practical — she's actually drafted model legislation that's been introduced in 3 states. She pushes back on pure academic approaches and insists on engagement with practitioners. Strong opinions about why the biosecurity community underestimates AI-enabled threats. Would be an excellent advisory board member.

name: Mark Thompson
interview_notes: Mark is brilliant technically but very academic in orientation. He understands the risks of open-source synbio tools but is primarily motivated by the research, not the safety implications. When pressed on how his screening tools would be implemented, he didn't have clear answers about deployment. Knows the tech cold but less aware of the policy landscape.

name: Lt. Gen. Patricia Morrison (Ret.)
institution: Center for a New American Security
key_publications: Various CNAS reports on biodefense modernization
interview_notes: Patricia brings 30 years of DoD experience including command of USAMRIID. She has an operational perspective that most policy researchers lack — she's actually run biodefense programs. Extremely well connected in the defense establishment. Pragmatic about what's achievable vs. aspirational. Her CNAS work bridges the academic-military divide unusually well.

name: Anil Gupta
key_publications: Lead author, WHO Biosafety Manual (5th edition)
interview_notes: Anil has unparalleled knowledge of international biosafety standards and how they're actually implemented (vs. on paper). He was candid about the gap between WHO guidance and country-level practice, especially in LMICs. He's looking for ways to improve technical assistance to countries building new biosafety capacity. Very interested in AI tools for risk assessment but skeptical of overpromising.
